In Vitro BSA Release Profile of DPAP Formulation

The
in
vitro
drug
release
proﬁle
of DPAP
formulation was
investigated over a pH range of 6–8 at 37 8C and 150 rpm shaking
speed. Figure 11 shows no initial burst release of drug molecules

after the ﬁrst 24 h at 37 8C, demonstrating the capability of
the

DPAP
formulation
to
harbor
and
retain
the
drug molecules

without premature diffusive
escape. The drug release proﬁles

proceeded with a
plateau for
the
different
pH systems. The
experimental results indicate that the release pattern of encapsu-

lated
drug molecules
is
highly
dependent
on
the molecular

properties of
the drug and degradation mechanism of
the DPAP
formulation. Hence, the presence of internal pores and composi-

tion of
the polymeric particles largely affect
the diffusion rate of

drug molecules to achieve a sustained release proﬁle whilst
the

degradation rate of
the outermost polymeric
layer deﬁnes
the

initial
burst
release
of
encapsulated
drugs
from the DPAP
formulation. Protonation and deprotonation of aptameric linkage

with the
PEI
polymeric
layer
can signiﬁcantly
inﬂuence
the
degradation rate of DPAP formulation at different pH levels. A

signiﬁcant protonation of PEI occurs under
acidic pH, which
impacts the linkages between the aptamer and PEI layer, resulting
in enhanced DPAP degradation.[33,34] The degree of protonation
and subsequent weakening of aptamer-PEI linkage decreases with
an increasing pH. Hence, the effect of pH is critical to maintaining
the desired release kinetics of DPAP formulation.
